case_name: L AND T INSURANCE CO LTD Versus BINDUDEVI SAMHARU YADAV & ORS.
court: High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad

compensation_breakdown.interest: 9% p.a. from date of filing the claim petition till realization
compensation_breakdown.notes: High Court dismissed insurer's appeal and directed disbursement of the award to the claimants after deducting court fee.

facts: On 19.05.2013 at about 1:00 pm on National Highway No.8 (Ahmedabad-Mumbai) the tempo DN-09-K-9486 driven by Sunil Samharu Yadav (loaded with yarn) had a punctured tyre and was parked on the left side while the driver was changing the wheel. A dumper truck GJ-18-X-9452 driven by respondent no.5 approached at excessive speed and dashed the tempo from behind; the deceased sustained grievous injuries and died. A claim under Section 166 of the Motor Vehicles Act was filed by the legal heirs seeking compensation; claimants had earlier also filed proceedings under the Workmen's Compensation Act against the deceased's employer and tempo insurer.
arguments_claimant: The claimants argued that the Workmen's Compensation petition was against the employer and the tempo's insurer and the MACT petition was against different tortfeasors (owner/driver/insurer of the dumper truck), so Section 167 of the Motor Vehicles Act did not bar the MACT claim. They also contended the insurance company failed to prove that the driving licence was fake and therefore failed to establish any fundamental breach to exonerate the insurer.
arguments_respondent: The appellant insurance company argued that the claimants had already received Rs.8,39,680/- under the Workmen's Compensation Act and therefore could not take dual benefit; reliance was placed on Section 167 MV Act and Supreme Court precedents. The insurer also contended the dumper truck driver did not possess an effective/valid driving licence (alleged to be fake) and thus the insurer ought to be exonerated from satisfying the award.
ratio_decidendi: "The gist of the aforesaid judgments referred hereinabove is that the bar of Section 167 of MV Act is applied only where the claimants seek compensation under the Motor Vehicles Act from the employer and insurer." The Court held that "the parties before the Commissioner Workmen's Compensation and the parties before the learned Motor Accident Claims Tribunal are different" and therefore "there is no nexus between the sets of parties before the both forums." Applying these principles, the Court concluded that Section 167 does not operate as a bar where the employer (sued under the Workmen's Compensation Act) is a different person from the tortfeasor sued under the Motor Vehicles Act, and thus claimants can maintain a claim against the tortfeasors other than the employer. The Court further held that the insurance company failed to prove the driving licence was fake and to show owner's knowledge of any fraud, and therefore it could not establish a fundamental breach of policy to escape liability.
final_order: The appeal filed by the insurer is dismissed. The Tribunal's award of Rs.12,12,400/- with interest at 9% p.a. from date of filing of the claim petition is confirmed and the Tribunal is directed to disburse the entire amount to the original claimants after deducting any deficit of court fee; connected civil application disposed.
summary: This is a High Court appeal by L and T Insurance challenging a MACT award of Rs.12,12,400/- (plus 9% interest) for the death of tempo driver Sunil Samharu Yadav in a rear-end collision with a dumper truck on 19.05.2013. The insurer argued the claim was barred by Section 167 because claimants had earlier obtained Workmen's Compensation and that the dumper driver held a fake licence. The Court held Section 167 does not apply where the parties before the Workmen's Compensation Commissioner and the MACT are different (employer vs tortfeasor) and found the insurer failed to prove the licence was fake or that the owner had knowledge; the appeal was dismissed and the award confirmed. Practically, insurers must prove both fakery of licence and owners knowledge to establish fundamental breach, and separate remedies against different parties may be maintained.
embedding_summary: L and T Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Bindudevi Samharu Yadav, High Court of Gujarat, C/FA/517/2019, judgment dated 19/01/2026. Accident on 19.05.2013 on NH-8: tempo DN-09-K-9486 (driver Sunil Samharu Yadav) hit from behind by dumper truck GJ-18-X-9452; deceased aged ~28, earning Rs.14,000/month. Tribunal awarded Rs.12,12,400/- + 9% p.a.; insurer appealed claiming prior Workmen's Compensation recovery (Rs.8,39,680/-) under Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923 and alleged fake/invalid driving licence. Key legal issues: applicability of Section 167 Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (doctrine of election/dual benefit) vs separate remedies under Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923 and Motor Vehicles Act; burden on insurer to prove fake licence and owner's knowledge to establish fundamental breach. Outcome: appeal dismissed, Tribunal award of Rs.12,12,400/- confirmed and directed to be disbursed to claimants; interest 9% from filing. Authorities relied: Mastan (2006) 2 SCC 641; Dyamavva (2013) 9 SCC 406; and multiple High Court precedents; also cited Hind Samachar Ltd. v. National Insurance Co. (2025 INSC 1204) on insurer's burden.
legal_principles: Section 167 Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 bar applies only where claimant seeks compensation under MV Act from the employer and insurer (same parties); not where parties differ between forums
Where parties before the Commissioner for Workmen's Compensation and the MACT are different (employer vs tortfeasor), the bar under Section 167 does not preclude a claim against the tortfeasor

Insurer seeking to escape liability on the ground of fake/invalid driving licence must produce cogent, corroborative documentary evidence and show owner's knowledge to establish a fundamental breach of policy
Amount recovered under Workmen's Compensation may be taken into account/deducted in certain circumstances, but deduction or bar depends on identity of parties and specific factual matrix
Tribunal's factual findings on evidence (e.g., validity of licence) are entitled to deference where insurer fails to produce convincing proof or relevant witnesses
sections_cited: Section 167 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988
Section 166 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988
Section 149(2) of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988
Section 3(5) of the Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923
Section 19 of the Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923
Section 14 of the Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923
Section 110A of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1939
Section 110-AA of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1939

Oriental Insurance Company Ltd. Vs. Dyamavva and Others, (2013) 9 SCC 406

Nasimbanu v. Ramjibhai Bachubhai Ahir, MANU/GJ/0048/2005
Gayatri Devi v. Tani Ram and Ors., AIR 1976 HP 75
Inspector General of Police v. Sayed Adam, MANU/MH/0352/1996
New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Smt. Bidami and Ors., 2009 SCC OnLine Raj 3440
The United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Anthony Selvam and Ors., 2014 SCC OnLine Mad 3506
Divisional Manager, New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Thiru. Astalingam and Ors., CMA (MD) No.443 of 2012
Hind Samachar Ltd. v. National Insurance Company Ltd. And Ors., 2025 INSC 1204
bm25_text: L AND T INSURANCE CO LTD Versus BINDUDEVI SAMHARU YADAV & ORS. High Court of Gujarat at Ahmedabad 2026 On 19.05.2013 at about 1:00 pm on National Highway No.8 (Ahmedabad-Mumbai) the tempo DN-09-K-9486 driven by Sunil Samharu Yadav (loaded with yarn) had a punctured tyre and was parked on the left side while the driver was changing the wheel. A dumper truck GJ-18-X-9452 driven by respondent no.5 approached at excessive speed and dashed the tempo from behind; the deceased sustained grievous injuries and died. A claim under Section 166 of the Motor Vehicles Act was filed by the legal heirs seeking compensation; claimants had earlier also filed proceedings under the Workmen's Compensation Act against the deceased's employer and tempo insurer. The claimants argued that the Workmen's Compensation petition was against the employer and the tempo's insurer and the MACT petition was against different tortfeasors (owner/driver/insurer of the dumper truck), so Section 167 of the Motor Vehicles Act did not bar the MACT claim. They also contended the insurance company failed to prove that the driving licence was fake and therefore failed to establish any fundamental breach to exonerate the insurer. The appellant insurance company argued that the claimants had already received Rs.8,39,680/- under the Workmen's Compensation Act and therefore could not take dual benefit; reliance was placed on Section 167 MV Act and Supreme Court precedents. The insurer also contended the dumper truck driver did not possess an effective/valid driving licence (alleged to be fake) and thus the insurer ought to be exonerated from satisfying the award. "The gist of the aforesaid judgments referred hereinabove is that the bar of Section 167 of MV Act is applied only where the claimants seek compensation under the Motor Vehicles Act from the employer and insurer." The Court held that "the parties before the Commissioner Workmen's Compensation and the parties before the learned Motor Accident Claims Tribunal are different" and therefore "there is no nexus between the sets of parties before the both forums." Applying these principles, the Court concluded that Section 167 does not operate as a bar where the employer (sued under the Workmen's Compensation Act) is a different person from the tortfeasor sued under the Motor Vehicles Act, and thus claimants can maintain a claim against the tortfeasors other than the employer. The Court further held that the insurance company failed to prove the driving licence was fake and to show owner's knowledge of any fraud, and therefore it could not establish a fundamental breach of policy to escape liability. The appeal filed by the insurer is dismissed. The Tribunal's award of Rs.12,12,400/- with interest at 9% p.a. from date of filing of the claim petition is confirmed and the Tribunal is directed to disburse the entire amount to the original claimants after deducting any deficit of court fee; connected civil application disposed. This is a High Court appeal by L and T Insurance challenging a MACT award of Rs.12,12,400/- (plus 9% interest) for the death of tempo driver Sunil Samharu Yadav in a rear-end collision with a dumper truck on 19.05.2013. The insurer argued the claim was barred by Section 167 because claimants had earlier obtained Workmen's Compensation and that the dumper driver held a fake licence. The Court held Section 167 does not apply where the parties before the Workmen's Compensation Commissioner and the MACT are different (employer vs tortfeasor) and found the insurer failed to prove the licence was fake or that the owner had knowledge; the appeal was dismissed and the award confirmed. Practically, insurers must prove both fakery of licence and owners knowledge to establish fundamental breach, and separate remedies against different parties may be maintained. L and T Insurance Co. Tribunal awarded Rs.12,12,400/- + 9% p.a.; insurer appealed claiming prior Workmen's Compensation recovery (Rs.8,39,680/-) under Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923 and alleged fake/invalid driving licence. Key legal issues: applicability of Section 167 Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (doctrine of election/dual benefit) vs separate remedies under Workmen's Compensation Act, 1923 and Motor Vehicles Act; burden on insurer to prove fake licence and owner's knowledge to establish fundamental breach. Outcome: appeal dismissed, Tribunal award of Rs.12,12,400/- confirmed and directed to be disbursed to claimants; interest 9% from filing.
